cuisine: Indian / South Asian (served in Malaysian and Singaporean contexts)
region_of_origin: South Asia (India); widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for deep-fried pappadum; A0CKP (flatbread/cracker category) used as closest defensible analogue. Reviewer should confirm against EFSA FoodEx2 browser.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pappadum as a specific food concept; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pappadum as an accompaniment to a Malaysian curry rice or Nasi Lemak meal consists of 2–3 pieces, weighing approximately 20 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Papadum, fried (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried papadum used as primary nutrient anchor. Input record values (energy 450 kcal, protein 17.5 g, fat 25 g, carbohydrate 40 g, sodium 1250 mg, dietary fibre 5 g, saturated fat 4 g, sugar 0.5 g) are broadly consistent with USDA data for deep-fried lentil-based crackers and are retained with medium confidence. ICMR-NIN (Indian Food Composition Tables 2017) also supports the protein and carbohydrate range for urad dal-based pappadum. Cholesterol confirmed at 0 mg (plant-based, no animal fat). Trans fat confirmed at 0 g. Vitamin C confirmed at 0 mg for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for deep-fried pappadum in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: urad dal (black gram lentil) preparations, which are consistently reported in the low-GI range (GI 38–55) in Atkinson et al. 2008 and Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed tables. Deep-frying increases fat content, which further slows gastric emptying and is expected to maintain or reduce GI relative to baked/roasted forms. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 8.0) / 100 = 3.84, rounded to 3.8. Serving carbohydrate: 40 g/100g × 20 g serving = 8.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1250 mg/100 g — more than double the 600 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Pappadum is contraindicated for individuals with hypertension; sodium at 1250 mg/100 g is critically elevated, and even a single standard serving of 20 g delivers approximately 250 mg sodium, representing a substantial proportion of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; this food should be avoided or replaced with a low-sodium roasted alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 40 g/100 g with low GI (~48) partially offset by dietary fibre 5 g/100 g and protein 17.5 g/100 g; deep-frying adds significant fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pappadum has a low estimated glycemic index (~48) due to its lentil-flour base and high protein and fibre content, which slow glucose absorption; however, carbohydrate load at 40 g/100 g and high fat from deep-frying require portion control — limit to 1–2 pieces (approximately 10–15 g) per meal and prefer roasted pappadum to reduce fat-mediated insulin resistance risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1250 mg/100 g and total fat 25 g/100 g from deep-frying; saturated fat 4 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-fried pappadum poses a high cardiovascular concern due to critically elevated sodium (1250 mg/100 g), which directly raises blood pressure, combined with total fat of 25 g/100 g from frying oil; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid this preparation and opt for roasted or microwaved pappadum with no added salt.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium 1250 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried pappadum is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the deep-frying process generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation, and sodium at 1250 mg/100 g significantly worsens RA-related tissue swelling; roasted low-sodium pappadum is the only acceptable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pappadum (also spelled papadum, papad, or poppadom) is a thin, crispy, disc-shaped Indian cracker traditionally made from black gram lentil flour (urad dal), salt, and spices such as black pepper and cumin. In Singapore and Malaysia, it is commonly served as an accompaniment to curry rice, Nasi Lemak, or banana-leaf rice meals. The deep-fried preparation produces a light, airy, and intensely crunchy texture with a savoury, mildly spiced flavour. Nutritionally, deep-fried pappadum is energy-dense at approximately 450 kcal per 100 g, with a notable protein content of 17.5 g/100 g from its lentil-flour base and significant fat at 25 g/100 g absorbed during frying. Dietary fibre at 5 g/100 g and a low estimated glycemic index (~48) mean that glucose release is relatively slow, making it moderately suitable for glycaemic management in small portions. However, sodium at 1250 mg/100 g is critically high, making this food unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. A standard serving of 2–3 pieces (approximately 20 g) delivers approximately 90 kcal, 3.5 g protein, 5 g fat, and 250 mg sodium. Roasted or microwaved pappadum is a significantly healthier alternative with substantially lower fat and, depending on formulation, lower sodium. Pappadum is naturally gluten-free, vegan, vegetarian, and halal when prepared without animal-derived frying oils.
common_user_queries: Can I eat pappadum with high blood pressure?
How much sodium is in pappadum?
Difference between fried and roasted pappadum calories
Is appalam the same as pappadum?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are broadly consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for deep-fried lentil-based crackers and ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for urad dal preparations. The input record was AI-generated with confidence 0.7 from a delivery platform source; values have been cross-validated against approved sources and retained where plausible. Sodium at 1250 mg/100 g is high but plausible for a salted, deep-fried product. GI is an analogous estimate from approved peer-reviewed sources. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown, potassium, and several micronutrients are not available in the consulted approved sources for this specific preparation and are set to -1.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for deep-fried pappadum not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or ICMR-NIN 2017 at the level of individual fatty acid fractions for this specific preparation. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these values are not reported in the primary approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN 2017) for deep-fried pappadum specifically; follow-up with ICMR-NIN full composition tables recommended. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for vegetable-oil deep-frying at standard temperatures per USDA data. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — pappadum is a plant-based product made from lentil flour with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — no vitamin C-containing ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on urad dal (black gram lentil) preparations from Atkinson et al. 2008 (University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for deep-fried pappadum was found in approved sources. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest defensible analogue for flatbread/cracker category; reviewer should verify against EFSA FoodEx2 browser. 8. Input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore, which reflects the serving context (Malaysian/Singaporean cuisine); region_of_origin corrected to South Asia (India) as the food's origin, with Singapore/Malaysia noted as the consumption context.
